title: scenario_trojan_infection
description: A Trojan horse disguised as a legitimate software installer establishes persistence, steals credentials, and opens a backdoor on the corporate network.

timeline.title: Suspicious Process Detected
timeline.description: EDR flags an unknown process launched by an HR employee after installing what appeared to be an HR software update.

timeline.title: Persistence Mechanisms Found
timeline.description: Analysis reveals registry run keys and a scheduled task created to ensure the Trojan survives reboots.

timeline.title: Credential Harvesting Activity
timeline.description: The Trojan is found to be logging keystrokes and scraping browser-saved passwords. Multiple credentials potentially compromised.

timeline.title: C2 Communication Identified
timeline.description: Firewall logs show beaconing traffic to a known C2 domain every 5 minutes. Attacker has command channel open.

timeline.title: Scope Assessment
timeline.description: Investigating how many machines received the malicious installer. Trojanized file traced to an unofficial download site.

timeline.title: Remediation Underway
timeline.description: Affected systems quarantined. Credentials rotated. Persistence mechanisms removed. C2 domains blocked at DNS.

timeline.title: Post-Incident Actions
timeline.description: Phishing awareness training updated. Software allowlisting policy reviewed. Vendor verification procedures enhanced.

node-1.title: Unknown Process Spawned by HR Software Installer
node-1.situation: An EDR alert fires on an HR workstation. A process named 'svcupdate32.exe' was spawned by an HR software installer downloaded from an unofficial mirror site. The employee says they clicked a Google ad to reach the download page.
node-1.question: What is your immediate response?

options.label: A
options.text: Isolate the workstation, kill the suspicious process, and pull the installer for analysis in a sandboxed environment.

options.label: B
options.text: Uninstall the HR software and reboot the machine — the problem should clear itself.

options.label: C
options.text: Whitelist the process in EDR to stop the alerts while you investigate over the next few days.

node-2.title: Persistence Mechanisms Discovered
node-2.situation: Sandbox analysis confirms a Trojan. It has written a registry run key and a scheduled task to execute on every login. It also attempted to disable Windows Defender. Additional machines may have received the same installer from the shared download.
node-2.question: How do you address the persistence and scope?

options.label: A
options.text: Search all endpoints via EDR for matching file hashes, registry keys, and scheduled tasks. Quarantine all matches and remove persistence mechanisms.

options.label: B
options.text: Remove persistence only from the known affected machine and wait to see if other alerts appear.

node-3.title: Credential Harvesting Confirmed
node-3.situation: Forensics shows the Trojan has been running for 3 days and logged keystrokes, including passwords to email, VPN, and a financial application. You do not know which credentials the attacker has already used.
node-3.question: How do you handle the credential compromise?

options.label: A
options.text: Force an immediate enterprise-wide password reset for all accounts, prioritizing VPN, email, and financial systems. Enable MFA everywhere.

options.label: B
options.text: Reset only the passwords of the confirmed affected employees to limit disruption.

node-4.title: C2 Beacon Detected
node-4.situation: Firewall logs reveal the Trojan has been beaconing to a C2 domain every 5 minutes. The attacker potentially has active access. The C2 domain resolves to an IP hosting several other known malicious domains.
node-4.question: How do you sever the attacker's access?

options.label: A
options.text: Block the C2 domain and IP at the DNS and firewall levels, notify threat intelligence feeds, and review firewall logs for any commands sent to the Trojan.

options.label: B
options.text: Leave the C2 channel open temporarily to monitor what the attacker is doing.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: All Trojan instances removed. Persistence mechanisms eliminated. Credentials rotated. C2 communication blocked. A software allowlisting policy is being drafted, and staff will receive updated phishing and safe-download awareness training.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
